author: jochembrouwer
content:
(2) can be fixed if we enshrine it, this is a tooling problem I would say. (1) is nasty from the UX side, but should not be a super big problem as it can be traced (just needs more resources)

I propose another option: which would be to deploy <@411317441688371202>'s contract using Nick's method (ideally we also mine some zeros in there so we dont PUSH20 if we call it from another contract (esp. the deployment byte cost) but instead PUSH19 or less) and use this address in <@792404665068158998>'s EIP which we then use to enshrine this new factory. 

We would need to depoy this before Glamsterdam or we would have to set a very high tx gas limit for this one to avoid these gas limit problems in the future.
The downside is for L2s which do not have this contract and which do not support non-EIP-155 txs is that they have to do an irregular state transition to use this new contract.
It would also now create this new contract factory which the community needs to start using.

No strong opinion here, I think enshrining the factory contract is something what we should do, whether it is the existing one or a new one, but this gives a strong signal from L1 and would also help the developer community a lot if they can be (somewhat certain) this contract exists on most/all chains
